steps.title: Identify the key signal in the researchers' argument
steps.content: The strongest signal is the researchers' statement: “Earth’s climate is changing in ways that challenge such classifications.” This directly indicates that the invasive classification may not hold as climate changes.

steps.title: Apply this to each answer choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — New Zealand was previously home to some rainbow lorikeets but they were outcompeted by invading species.: Introduces unsupported history — that rainbow lorikeets were previously present in New Zealand and were outcompeted. The passage contains no such information. Eliminated.
  • Choice B — Even if Earth's climate doesn't change in the way scientists predict, the rainbow lorikeet will likely establish itself in New Zealand.: Reverses the passage’s premise by assuming climate change does not affect classifications. This directly contradicts the researchers’ emphasis on climate change challenging existing labels. Eliminated.
  • Choice C — Labels like the one that New Zealand has applied to the rainbow lorikeet reflect environmental conditions that may not persist.: Correctly captures the logical consequence — if climate will change environmental conditions, then the invasive label reflects conditions that may not persist. Supported.
  • Choice D — it's useful at present for New Zealand to distinguish between invasive and native species in some instances but not in the case of the rainbow lorikeet.: Proposes selectively applying invasive labels to some species but not others. The passage questions the classification framework generally, not selectively. Eliminated.
This matches — C is correct.
